name: Tirzepatide
description: Tirzepatide is a dual GIP and GLP-1 receptor agonist developed for type 2 diabetes and weight management. It is the first approved dual agonist, showing superior efficacy in glycemic control and weight reduction compared to selective GLP-1 agonists.
mechanism: Dual agonist at GIP and GLP-1 receptors. GIP enhances insulin secretion and may counteract GLP-1-mediated nausea, while GLP-1 slows gastric emptying and promotes satiety.
longDescription:
## Overview

Tirzepatide is a groundbreaking dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist that has transformed the landscape of metabolic research. Marketed under brand names including Mounjaro and Zepbound, and also identified by the research code LY3298176, this synthetic peptide represents the first approved therapy to simultaneously target both incretin pathways. Its unique mechanism sets it apart from selective GLP-1 agonists such as semaglutide by harnessing the complementary actions of GIP — which enhances insulin secretion and may counteract GLP-1-mediated nausea — alongside GLP-1's well-established effects on gastric emptying and satiety. Researchers in the UK and worldwide are investigating tirzepatide for type 2 diabetes management, weight loss research, metabolic health optimisation, and emerging applications in non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD/NASH). Its superior efficacy in both glycaemic control and weight reduction has made it one of the most sought-after research peptides for metabolic studies.

## Mechanism of Action

Tirzepatide functions as a balanced dual agonist at both the GIP receptor (GIPR) and the GLP-1 receptor (GLP-1R). GIP activation promotes glucose-dependent insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells and may improve the tolerability profile by counteracting some of the gastrointestinal side effects typically associated with GLP-1 agonism. Meanwhile, GLP-1 receptor activation slows gastric emptying, enhances satiety through central nervous system pathways, suppresses glucagon release, and promotes insulin secretion in a glucose-dependent manner. This coordinated dual-receptor engagement produces additive and potentially synergistic effects on weight loss and glycaemic control, exceeding what is achievable with either pathway alone. The peptide has a molecular mass of approximately 4813.5 g/mol and a sequence that incorporates a C-terminal amide for improved stability.

## Research & Clinical Data

Clinical trials have demonstrated that tirzepatide achieves superior reductions in HbA1c and body weight compared to selective GLP-1 receptor agonists. In the SURPASS programme, tirzepatide at doses up to 15 mg once weekly produced HbA1c reductions of up to 2.4% and mean weight loss of up to 12.9 kg (approximately 13.5% of baseline body weight). The SURMOUNT-1 trial in patients with obesity but without diabetes reported mean weight reductions of up to 22.5% at the highest dose over 72 weeks, setting a new benchmark for pharmacologically induced weight loss. Researchers are also exploring its potential in NAFLD/NASH through the SYNERGY-NASH trial, where improvements in liver histology and fibrosis have been observed.

## Dosage & Administration

In research settings, tirzepatide is typically administered via subcutaneous injection once weekly. Common research dosages follow a dose-escalation protocol beginning at 2.5 mg per week and increasing stepwise through 5 mg, 7.5 mg, 10 mg, 12.5 mg, up to a maximum studied dose of 15 mg per week. The half-life of approximately 5 days supports once-weekly dosing. The lyophilised powder should be reconstituted with bacteriostatic water or sterile water for injection prior to use, and solutions should be stored under refrigeration at 2–8°C.

## Safety & Legal

Tirzepatide is intended for research purposes only and is not approved for human consumption outside of regulated clinical settings. Common research reported effects include transient nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, and decreased appetite, particularly during dose escalation. Long-term safety beyond 72 weeks in obesity trials continues to be evaluated. Products should be stored as lyophilised powder at -20°C for extended stability and protected from light and moisture.


The discovery and development of tirzepatide represents a landmark achievement in rational drug design. By engineering a single molecule that activates both the GIP and GLP-1 pathways with balanced potency, researchers have demonstrated that dual incretin receptor agonism produces metabolic benefits that exceed the sum of its parts. The GIP component is particularly interesting as early research had suggested GIP's role in obesity was limited due to apparent GIP resistance in insulin-resistant states — yet tirzepatide has overturned this paradigm.

The SURMOUNT programme is the most comprehensive evaluation of tirzepatide in obesity to date. In SURMOUNT-1, tirzepatide at 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg produced mean weight losses of 16%, 21.4%, and 22.5% respectively at 72 weeks, compared to 2.4% with placebo. Importantly, these effects were achieved with a progressive dose escalation protocol that improved tolerability. The SURPASS programme in type 2 diabetes demonstrated that tirzepatide at all doses outperformed semaglutide 1 mg in HbA1c reduction and weight loss, establishing it as the most effective incretin-based therapy currently studied.

For researchers planning metabolic studies, tirzepatide's 5-day half-life means that once-weekly dosing in preclinical models is appropriate. The peptide should be reconstituted with bacteriostatic water at a concentration that allows the desired weekly dose in a reasonable injection volume (typically 0.1–0.5 mL). Stability after reconstitution is approximately 14 days under refrigeration (2–8°C). Unreconstituted lyophilised powder can be stored at -20°C for up to 24 months.

Emerging research directions include tirzepatide's effects on cardiovascular outcomes, liver histology in NASH, and potential applications in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). The SUMMIT cardiovascular outcomes trial is currently evaluating tirzepatide in patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) and obesity, which could expand its therapeutic footprint significantly.

name: Semaglutide
description: Semaglutide is a long-acting GLP-1 receptor agonist approved for type 2 diabetes and chronic weight management. It shares 94% sequence homology with human GLP-1 with modifications for extended half-life.
mechanism: GLP-1 receptor agonist that stimulates insulin secretion, suppresses glucagon release, slows gastric emptying, and reduces appetite through central mechanisms.
longDescription:
## Overview

Semaglutide is a long-acting glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist that has become one of the most extensively researched peptides in metabolic medicine. Available under brand names such as Ozempic, Wegovy, and Rybelsus, semaglutide shares 94% sequence homology with native human GLP-1, with strategic modifications that dramatically extend its circulating half-life and enhance its resistance to enzymatic degradation by dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4). Originally developed and approved for type 2 diabetes management, semaglutide gained worldwide attention following landmark trials demonstrating remarkable efficacy in chronic weight management. UK researchers have been at the forefront of investigating its applications, from glycaemic control to cardiovascular outcomes and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. Its well-characterised safety profile and once-weekly dosing schedule make it a cornerstone comparator in metabolic research.

## Mechanism of Action

Semaglutide acts as a potent and selective agonist at the GLP-1 receptor, a class B G-protein-coupled receptor expressed on pancreatic beta cells, gastric mucosa, and various regions of the central nervous system. Upon activation, it stimulates glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppresses glucagon release from pancreatic alpha cells, and delays gastric emptying, collectively reducing postprandial glucose excursions. At the central level, semaglutide activates GLP-1 receptors in the hypothalamus and brainstem to promote satiety and reduce caloric intake. The fatty acid side chain at Lys26 facilitates albumin binding, which slows renal clearance and extends the half-life to approximately 7 days, enabling once-weekly subcutaneous administration.

## Research & Clinical Data

The SUSTAIN clinical trial programme established semaglutide's efficacy in type 2 diabetes, with 1.0 mg once-weekly achieving HbA1c reductions of up to 1.8% and weight loss of 4–6 kg. The landmark STEP programme in obesity demonstrated that semaglutide 2.4 mg (Wegovy) produced mean weight reductions of approximately 14.9% over 68 weeks combined with lifestyle intervention — a result that reshaped weight management guidelines globally. The SELECT cardiovascular outcomes trial further showed a 20% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events in patients with overweight or obesity and established cardiovascular disease, independent of baseline HbA1c.

## Dosage & Administration

In research settings, semaglutide is administered via subcutaneous injection once weekly. Common dosages include 0.25 mg, 0.5 mg, 1.0 mg, 1.7 mg, and 2.4 mg per week, with gradual dose escalation recommended to assess tolerability. The lyophilised powder requires reconstitution with bacteriostatic water prior to use. For those looking to buy semaglutide UK supply is available from multiple research vendors; always verify product purity (≥98%) and storage conditions.

## Safety & Legal

Semaglutide is strictly for research purposes and not for human use. Common research reported events include transient nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, and constipation, which are usually dose-dependent and diminish over time. More serious but rare concerns include pancreatitis, gallbladder disease, and retinopathy complications noted in the SUSTAIN-6 trial. The peptide should be stored at -20°C as lyophilised powder and at 2–8°C after reconstitution for up to 14 days.


The clinical development of semaglutide is a story of iterative peptide engineering that transformed a short-lived native hormone into a once-weekly therapeutic powerhouse. The key innovation was acylation — attaching a C18 fatty diacid side chain to Lys26 via a hydrophilic spacer — which enables albumin binding and extends the half-life from minutes to approximately 7 days. Additionally, the substitution of Ala8 with the non-natural amino acid Aib (α-aminoisobutyric acid) provides resistance to DPP-4 degradation while maintaining full GLP-1 receptor potency.

The SELECT trial (Semaglutide Effects on Cardiovascular Outcomes in People with Overweight or Obesity) reported in 2023 was a landmark study that demonstrated semaglutide 2.4 mg reduced major adverse cardiovascular events by 20% in patients with pre-existing cardiovascular disease and overweight or obesity, independent of baseline HbA1c. This finding established semaglutide as the first weight-loss therapy to demonstrate cardiovascular benefit in a dedicated outcomes trial, reshaping clinical guidelines for obesity management.

For research applications, semaglutide is typically administered once weekly at doses ranging from 0.25 mg (starting dose for tolerability assessment) to 2.4 mg (full therapeutic dose in obesity studies). The peptide should be reconstituted with bacteriostatic water and can be stored under refrigeration for up to 14 days. Researchers should note that semaglutide's dose-response curve is steep, and gradual dose escalation is essential to assess gastrointestinal tolerability in study subjects.

Ongoing research includes semaglutide in NAFLD/NASH (the ESSENCE trial), Alzheimer's disease (the EVOKE and EVOKE Plus trials), and chronic kidney disease (the FLOW trial, which was stopped early for efficacy). These indications may expand semaglutide's role beyond metabolic health into broader age-related disease prevention.

name: BPC-157
description: BPC-157 is a synthetic pentadecapeptide derived from a protein found in human gastric juice. It is extensively studied for its regenerative properties on various tissues including the gastrointestinal tract, muscles, tendons, and ligaments.
mechanism: Promotes angiogenesis, upregulates growth factors (VEGF, FGF), modulates nitric oxide system, and accelerates wound healing through multiple signaling pathways.
longDescription:
## Overview

BPC-157, also known as Body Protection Compound 157 or PL 14736, is a synthetic pentadecapeptide (15 amino acids) derived from a protective protein found naturally in human gastric juice. Since its discovery, BPC-157 has become one of the most extensively studied regenerative peptides in the world, with research spanning gastrointestinal healing, tendon and ligament repair, muscle recovery, bone healing, and inflammatory bowel disease. What makes BPC-157 particularly remarkable is its apparent ability to accelerate healing across multiple tissue types through a coordinated mechanism involving angiogenesis, growth factor upregulation, and modulation of the nitric oxide system. UK-based researchers and those in the sports medicine community have shown particular interest in BPC-157 for its potential applications in soft tissue injury recovery and gut health. Its well-documented safety profile in animal models has made it a staple in regenerative peptide research.

## Mechanism of Action

BPC-157 exerts its regenerative effects through a multi-faceted mechanism that centres on angiogenesis promotion and growth factor modulation. It upregulates vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and fibroblast growth factor (FGF), stimulating new blood vessel formation that supplies healing tissues with oxygen and nutrients. The peptide also modulates the nitric oxide (NO) system, which plays a crucial role in the inflammatory and proliferative phases of wound healing. At the molecular level, BPC-157 upregulates early growth response factor-1 (Egr-1), which coordinates the expression of multiple growth factors and cytokines involved in tissue repair. It also protects gastrointestinal mucosa by increasing blood flow, mucus production, and epithelial cell migration while reducing oxidative stress and inflammation.

## Research & Clinical Data

BPC-157 has been studied extensively in preclinical models of gastrointestinal injury, demonstrating accelerated healing of gastric and duodenal ulcers, inflammatory bowel disease models, and chemotherapy-induced mucositis. In musculoskeletal research, BPC-157 has shown accelerated healing of transected Achilles tendons, improved ligament integrity after injury, and enhanced muscle regeneration following contusion and crush injuries. Studies also report improved healing of bone fractures, including non-union defects, and protection against ischaemia-reperfusion injury in multiple organ systems.

## Dosage & Administration

In research protocols, BPC-157 is typically administered via subcutaneous injection at doses of 200–400 mcg per day for systemic effects, or up to 500 mcg twice daily for more intensive protocols targeting significant injury or gastrointestinal conditions. The peptide has a short half-life (fast clearance), which supports daily or twice-daily dosing schedules. Reconstitution with bacteriostatic water is standard, and the solution should be refrigerated at 2–8°C and used within 7–14 days.

## Safety & Legal

BPC-157 is intended for laboratory research purposes only and is not approved for human consumption. Preclinical studies have reported a favourable safety profile with no observed toxicity at therapeutic doses. However, long-term safety data in humans remain limited. The lyophilised powder should be stored at -20°C for optimal stability and protected from light and moisture. Researchers should source from reputable suppliers who provide ≥99% purity verification.


The story of BPC-157's discovery traces back to observations that human gastric juice contained factors capable of protecting the gastrointestinal tract from injury. Researchers at the University of Zagreb in Croatia identified and characterised BPC-157 as a stable 15-amino acid fragment of a larger body protection compound (BPC) found in gastric juice. The peptide's remarkable stability in the acidic gastric environment and its ability to survive enzymatic degradation hinted at its therapeutic potential for oral administration — though the injectable route remains the gold standard for systemic research.

One of the most striking aspects of BPC-157 research is the breadth of tissue types in which it promotes healing. Beyond gastrointestinal ulcer healing, BPC-157 has demonstrated efficacy in tendon-to-bone healing (a notoriously difficult orthopaedic problem), peripheral nerve regeneration, liver and kidney protection from toxic insults, and even bone healing in non-union fracture models. The peptide appears to upregulate the expression of multiple growth factors simultaneously, creating a coordinated healing response rather than targeting a single pathway — which may explain its broad tissue-specific efficacy.

For research protocols, BPC-157's fast clearance (plasma half-life is short, though tissue retention may be longer) supports daily or twice-daily administration. Typical reconstitution uses bacteriostatic water, and the peptide is stable for approximately 14 days when refrigerated. Some researchers use a sub-Q injection near the site of injury for localised effects, though systemic administration has proven effective in most models.

The question of whether BPC-157 is available in the UK for legitimate research is important for investigators. The peptide is not approved for human use by the MHRA, but it is legally sold for research purposes by UK-based suppliers. Researchers should always verify that their supplier provides third-party HPLC and mass spectrometry analysis to confirm identity and purity.

faq.answer: BPC-157 is primarily researched for tissue regeneration and healing applications including gastrointestinal repair, tendon and ligament recovery, muscle healing, bone fracture repair, and inflammatory bowel disease. Its broad regenerative properties make it one of the most versatile peptides in research.

name: TB-500
description: TB-500 is the synthetic version of Thymosin Beta-4, a naturally occurring peptide that plays a crucial role in wound healing, cell migration, and tissue regeneration. It is one of the most studied regenerative peptides.
mechanism: Acts as an actin-sequestering protein, promoting cell migration and differentiation. Upregulates angiogenesis through VEGF and FGF pathways.
longDescription:
## Overview

TB-500 is the synthetic version of Thymosin Beta-4 (Tβ4), a naturally occurring 43-amino acid peptide that is one of the most abundant and important wound-healing factors in the human body. As an actin-sequestering protein, Tβ4 plays a fundamental role in cell migration, differentiation, and tissue remodelling — processes essential for all phases of wound healing. TB-500 has garnered significant attention in regenerative medicine research for its broad therapeutic potential, from accelerating wound closure and cardiac repair following myocardial infarction to reducing inflammation, promoting hair growth, and supporting eye health. Found in virtually all human cells, Tβ4's central role in cytoskeletal dynamics makes it a uniquely versatile investigational peptide. UK researchers in sports medicine and tissue engineering have shown particular interest in TB-500 for its ability to promote angiogenesis and modulate the inflammatory response in musculoskeletal injuries.

## Mechanism of Action

TB-500 functions primarily as an actin-sequestering protein, binding to monomeric G-actin and preventing its polymerisation into F-actin filaments. This dynamic regulation of the actin cytoskeleton is essential for cell motility, migration, and shape changes during wound healing and tissue remodelling. Beyond its actin-binding activity, TB-500 upregulates the expression of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and fibroblast growth factor (FGF), stimulating angiogenesis and providing the vascular supply necessary for tissue repair. It also modulates inflammatory signalling by downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines (including TNF-α and IL-1β) while upregulating anti-inflammatory mediators. Additionally, TB-500 promotes cell survival by activating the Akt signalling pathway and reducing apoptosis in stressed tissues.

## Research & Clinical Data

TB-500 has been investigated in numerous preclinical models of tissue injury and repair. In cardiac research, Tβ4 administration following myocardial infarction has been shown to improve cardiac function, reduce scar size, and promote cardiomyocyte survival. Wound healing studies demonstrate accelerated full-thickness wound closure with improved tensile strength and reduced scar formation. Ocular research has explored TB-500 for corneal repair, showing enhanced re-epithelialisation and reduced inflammation. In the UK, research into TB-500 for tendon and ligament injuries has been particularly active, with studies showing improved functional recovery after Achilles tendon rupture and reduced fibrosis in muscle injury models.

## Dosage & Administration

Common research dosages for TB-500 range from 2.5–5 mg per week, sometimes with a loading protocol of 10 mg in the first week followed by 2.5–5 mg weekly maintenance. The peptide has a relatively short half-life of 4–6 hours, yet its biological effects can persist due to its intracellular mechanism of action. It is typically administered via subcutaneous or intramuscular injection after reconstitution with bacteriostatic water. Post-reconstitution solutions should be stored at 2–8°C.

## Safety & Legal

TB-500 is intended for research purposes only. Preclinical studies suggest a favourable safety profile, though comprehensive human safety data are limited. The peptide should be stored as lyophilised powder at -20°C and protected from light and moisture. Researchers should ensure they purchase from vendors providing third-party purity verification (≥99%).


Thymosin Beta-4 was first isolated from calf thymus in 1981 and was initially studied for its immunomodulatory properties. It was later discovered that Tβ4 is the major actin-sequestering protein in most eukaryotic cells, present at concentrations of 0.1–0.5 mM in the cytoplasm. This fundamental role in cytoskeletal dynamics places Tβ4 at the centre of cell migration, a process essential for embryonic development, immune surveillance, and tissue repair. The synthetic version, TB-500, is identical in sequence to the naturally occurring human Tβ4.

Research into TB-500's cardiac protective effects has been particularly promising. In animal models of myocardial infarction, Tβ4 administration at the time of reperfusion reduced infarct size by up to 50% and improved left ventricular function. The mechanism appears to involve activation of the Akt survival pathway in cardiomyocytes and endothelial cells, reduced oxidative stress, and enhanced recruitment of cardiac progenitor cells to the injured area. These findings have led to interest in TB-500 as a potential therapeutic for ischaemic heart disease.

The synergy between TB-500 and BPC-157 is a frequently discussed topic in regenerative peptide research. While both peptides promote angiogenesis and wound healing, their mechanisms are complementary rather than redundant. TB-500's actin-sequestering activity promotes cell migration, while BPC-157's growth factor upregulation creates a pro-healing microenvironment. Many research protocols combine both peptides for enhanced tissue repair outcomes.

In terms of practical research use, TB-500 is typically dosed at 2.5–5 mg per week, with a loading dose of 10 mg in the first week for acute injury models. The peptide has good stability when stored as lyophilised powder at -20°C and should be protected from light and moisture. TB-500 is available from UK research suppliers and should always be verified by independent HPLC analysis.

Its fundamental role in actin binding and cell migration makes it relevant to virtually all tissue repair processes.

faq.answer: TB-500 works primarily as an actin-sequestering protein, binding to G-actin and regulating cytoskeletal dynamics essential for cell migration. It also upregulates VEGF and FGF for angiogenesis, modulates cytokine production to reduce inflammation, and promotes cell survival through Akt signalling.

faq.answer: Typical research protocols use 2.5–5 mg per week, sometimes with an initial loading dose of 10 mg. Due to its 4–6 hour half-life but persistent biological effects, once or twice weekly dosing is common.

name: Melanotan II
description: Melanotan II is a synthetic analog of the naturally occurring melanocyte-stimulating hormone (α-MSH). It stimulates melanogenesis for skin tanning and has been studied for its effects on sexual function.
mechanism: Non-selective agonist at melanocortin receptors (MC1R-MC5R). MC1R activation induces melanin production; MC4R activation affects appetite and sexual function.
longDescription:
## Overview

Melanotan II (MT-II) is a synthetic cyclic heptapeptide analogue of α-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (α-MSH), the body's natural pigment-regulating hormone. Developed initially at the University of Arizona as a potential sunless tanning agent and photoprotective therapy, Melanotan II has become one of the most widely known research peptides. It acts as a non-selective melanocortin receptor agonist, activating all five melanocortin receptor subtypes (MC1R–MC5R). While its most visible effect is stimulation of melanogenesis (skin pigmentation) through MC1R activation, the compound also influences appetite regulation via MC4R and, interestingly, sexual function through central melanocortin pathways — the same mechanism that led to the development of its analogue bremelanotide (PT-141). UK researchers continue to study MT-II for applications including erythropoietic protoporphyria, sexual dysfunction, and understanding melanocortin biology.

## Mechanism of Action

Melanotan II functions as a non-selective agonist at all five melanocortin receptor subtypes. Activation of MC1R on melanocytes stimulates the cAMP signalling cascade, upregulating tyrosinase enzyme activity and driving the production of eumelanin (dark pigment) independently of UV exposure. MC4R activation in the hypothalamus regulates appetite and energy homeostasis, producing an anorexigenic effect. MC3R and MC4R signalling also modulate sexual arousal and erectile function through central nervous system pathways involving oxytocinergic neurons. MC2R is primarily the ACTH receptor and is less relevant to Melanotan II's primary effects. The cyclic structure and Nle substitution confer improved stability and potency compared to native α-MSH.

## Research & Clinical Data

Clinical studies have demonstrated that Melanotan II effectively induces tanning in fair-skinned individuals, with a 10–14 day loading phase producing visible pigmentation that persists for weeks. Research in erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP) has shown that MT-II-induced melanisation can improve light tolerance and quality of life. Studies on sexual function report improvements in erectile function and libido in both men and women, mediated through central melanocortin pathways. However, development has been limited by side effects including nausea, flushing, and spontaneous erections.

## Dosage & Administration

Typical research dosages for Melanotan II range from 0.5–1 mg per day, with a common loading protocol of 0.5 mg per day for 10–14 days to establish pigmentation, followed by maintenance dosing of 1–2 times per week. The peptide has a short plasma half-life of 30–60 minutes, but melanogenic effects persist for days due to sustained tyrosinase upregulation. Subcutaneous administration is standard after reconstitution with bacteriostatic water.

## Safety & Legal

Melanotan II is strictly for research purposes. Common effects reported in studies include nausea, facial flushing, spontaneous erections, appetite suppression, and potential darkening of pre-existing naevi (moles). The long-term safety profile is not well established. The lyophilised powder should be stored at -20°C and protected from light. UK researchers should be aware that Melanotan II is not licensed for human use and should only be obtained from reputable research suppliers.


Melanotan II was originally developed at the University of Arizona as part of a research programme aimed at creating a sunless tanning agent that could reduce skin cancer risk by decreasing UV exposure. The peptide's ability to induce melanogenesis without UV radiation was demonstrated in early clinical studies at the Arizona Cancer Center, where fair-skinned volunteers developed visible pigmentation after 10–14 days of daily administration. This mechanism — activating MC1R to stimulate eumelanin production — provided the first practical alternative to UV-induced tanning.

The melanocortin system extends far beyond pigmentation. MC4R activation in the hypothalamus regulates energy homeostasis, and MC3R and MC4R activation in the spinal cord and brainstem modulates sexual function. Bremelanotide (PT-141), the active metabolite of Melanotan II that lacks the melanogenic effects, was FDA-approved in 2019 for hypoactive sexual desire disorder in premenopausal women. The development of analogues that separate these effects remains an active area of medicinal chemistry research.

Clinical research in erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP), a rare genetic disorder causing severe photosensitivity, has demonstrated that Melanotan II-induced melanisation can dramatically improve light tolerance. EPP patients who previously could tolerate only minutes of sunlight exposure were able to spend hours outdoors after treatment — a life-changing improvement. This application highlights the therapeutic potential of melanocortin agonists beyond cosmetic tanning.

For research use, Melanotan II requires careful handling. The peptide is sensitive to light degradation and should be protected during reconstitution and storage. Common doses of 0.5–1 mg per day during the loading phase should be administered subcutaneously. Researchers should be aware that dosing errors (particularly overdosing) can cause significant nausea, so precise reconstitution and measurement are essential. UK researchers can obtain Melanotan II from specialised peptide suppliers who provide purity documentation.

faq.answer: Melanotan II is primarily researched for UV-independent skin tanning (melanogenesis), sexual dysfunction (erectile function and libido), appetite regulation, and erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP). It is a synthetic analogue of α-MSH that activates melanocortin receptors.

faq.answer: MT-II activates MC1R receptors on melanocytes, stimulating cAMP signalling and upregulating tyrosinase, the key enzyme in melanin production. This drives eumelanin synthesis independently of UV exposure, producing a sunless tan that develops over 10–14 days.

name: GHRP-2
description: GHRP-2 is a synthetic hexapeptide that stimulates the release of growth hormone from the pituitary gland. It is one of the most potent GHRPs and is widely used in research.
mechanism: Ghrelin receptor (GHSR-1a) agonist. Stimulates GH release from somatotrophs and may also increase cortisol and prolactin.
longDescription:
## Overview

GHRP-2 (Growth Hormone Releasing Peptide-2), also known as pralmorelin, is a synthetic hexapeptide that potently stimulates the release of growth hormone from the anterior pituitary gland. As one of the most potent members of the growth hormone-releasing peptide family, GHRP-2 has been extensively studied for its ability to significantly elevate GH levels in a dose-dependent manner. Discovered in the 1990s through systematic structure-activity studies of enkephalin-related peptides, GHRP-2 acts through a mechanism distinct from GHRH, making it a valuable tool in both diagnostic testing for GH deficiency and research into muscle growth, anti-aging, and fat metabolism. UK researchers have utilised GHRP-2 extensively in endocrine research and sports science, particularly in combination protocols with GHRH analogues such as CJC-1295 or sermorelin for enhanced GH pulse generation.

## Mechanism of Action

GHRP-2 functions as a potent agonist at the ghrelin receptor (growth hormone secretagogue receptor type 1a, GHSR-1a), a G-protein-coupled receptor expressed primarily in the pituitary gland and hypothalamus. Activation of GHSR-1a stimulates phospholipase C and intracellular calcium mobilisation in pituitary somatotrophs, triggering the exocytosis of pre-synthesised GH granules. Unlike GHRH, which stimulates GH synthesis and release, GHRP-2 primarily amplifies the releasable GH pool and synergises with GHRH signalling to produce a robust GH pulse. Additionally, GHRP-2 may stimulate cortisol and prolactin release, and it influences appetite regulation through its ghrelin-mimetic action at the hypothalamic level.

## Research & Clinical Data

GHRP-2 has been used clinically in Japan as a diagnostic agent for GH deficiency (as pralmorelin), where it demonstrates reliable stimulation of GH release with a favourable safety profile. Research studies report that GHRP-2 can elevate GH levels several-fold above baseline, with peak concentrations occurring 15–30 minutes after administration. Combined administration with GHRH or GHRH analogues produces a synergistic effect, generating GH pulses comparable to or exceeding those seen with insulin-induced hypoglycaemia. Research into its potential for anti-aging, muscle preservation, and metabolic health continues.

## Dosage & Administration

Common research dosages for GHRP-2 range from 100–300 mcg per day, often divided into 2–3 daily doses of 100–200 mcg each. Due to its short half-life of 30–60 minutes, multiple daily injections are typically employed to maintain growth hormone pulsatility. GHRP-2 is often used in combination with a GHRH analogue (e.g., CJC-1295 without DAC or sermorelin) for a synergistic GH pulse. Subcutaneous administration is standard after reconstitution with bacteriostatic water.

## Safety & Legal

GHRP-2 is for research purposes only. Reported effects may include transient increases in appetite (ghrelin-mimetic effect), as well as potential elevations in cortisol and prolactin. Long-term effects of sustained GH elevation are not fully characterised. The lyophilised powder should be stored at -20°C for optimal stability.


The GHRP family evolved from enkephalin-derived peptides discovered by Drs. Bowers and Momany at Tulane University in the 1970s and 1980s. These small peptides were found to stimulate GH release through a receptor distinct from the GHRH receptor — later identified as the ghrelin receptor (GHSR-1a). GHRP-2 emerged as the most potent member of this class through systematic structure-activity relationship studies, with a potency approximately 2–3 times greater than GHRP-6 and a more favourable side effect profile.

The pulsatile nature of GH secretion is critical for its biological effects. Unlike continuous GH elevation (which can cause insulin resistance and acromegaly-like effects), natural GH pulses produce optimal metabolic benefits. GHRP-2 amplifies the amplitude of endogenous GH pulses rather than creating a constant elevation, which is thought to preserve the physiological advantages of pulsatile secretion. This is particularly relevant when GHRP-2 is combined with a GHRH analogue, which enhances GH pulse amplitude synergistically.

In Japan, GHRP-2 (pralmorelin) is approved as a diagnostic agent for GH deficiency. The test involves measuring GH levels after a single GHRP-2 injection and has largely replaced the insulin tolerance test as the gold standard for GH deficiency diagnosis due to its superior safety profile. This clinical experience provides confidence in the peptide's tolerability for research applications.

For researchers planning GHRP-2 protocols, the short half-life (30–60 minutes) necessitates careful timing of doses relative to meals and time of day. GH pulsatility is highest at night and during fasting states, so morning doses on an empty stomach or pre-bed administration are common. The peptide is typically reconstituted with bacteriostatic water and should be used within 7–14 days when refrigerated. UK researchers can source GHRP-2 from reputable peptide vendors who provide purity certification.

faq.answer: Typical research dosages range from 100–300 mcg per day, often divided into 2–3 doses. Due to its short half-life of 30–60 minutes, multiple daily administrations are common. It is frequently combined with a GHRH analogue for enhanced GH pulse generation.

name: AOD-9604
description: AOD-9604 is a synthetic peptide fragment of human growth hormone (amino acids 177-191). It was developed specifically for fat loss without affecting blood sugar or growth hormone levels.
mechanism: Mimics the lipolytic (fat-burning) action of hGH without the anti-insulin effects. Stimulates lipolysis in adipose tissue while inhibiting lipogenesis.
longDescription:
## Overview

AOD-9604 (Anti-Obesity Drug 9604), also known as HGH Fragment 177-191, is a synthetic 15-amino acid peptide fragment corresponding to amino acids 177–191 of the human growth hormone molecule. Developed specifically for fat loss research, AOD-9604 represents a targeted approach to metabolic modulation that separates the lipolytic (fat-burning) properties of hGH from its anti-insulin and growth-promoting effects. This selective action makes AOD-9604 a uniquely interesting research tool for studying adipose tissue metabolism, as it stimulates lipolysis (fat breakdown) and inhibits lipogenesis (fat storage) without affecting blood sugar levels or promoting tissue growth. UK metabolic researchers have investigated AOD-9604's potential in obesity treatment and weight management protocols, with particular interest in its safety profile and ability to target stubborn fat deposits.

## Mechanism of Action

AOD-9604 mimics the lipolytic action of full-length human growth hormone by binding to the GH receptor and activating the JAK2/STAT5 signalling pathway specifically in adipose tissue. The fragment corresponds to the active site of hGH responsible for its fat-burning effects, while lacking the domain responsible for insulin antagonism and IGF-1 stimulation. This results in stimulation of hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL), which breaks down stored triglycerides into free fatty acids for oxidation in mitochondria, while simultaneously downregulating lipoprotein lipase (LPL) activity to reduce fat storage. The peptide also stimulates β-oxidation and thermogenesis in adipocytes, increasing energy expenditure. Crucially, because it lacks the GH domain for diabetogenic activity, AOD-9604 does not impair insulin sensitivity or elevate blood glucose.

## Research & Clinical Data

Clinical trials of AOD-9604 have demonstrated statistically significant fat loss, particularly in abdominal and visceral adipose tissue, without adverse effects on glucose metabolism. A double-blind, placebo-controlled study of 300 overweight subjects reported significant reductions in total body fat and waist circumference over 12 weeks with AOD-9604 treatment compared to placebo. Preclinical studies have confirmed its ability to stimulate lipolysis in both white and brown adipose tissue, and to reduce body weight gain in diet-induced obesity models without affecting food intake. Research into optimal dosing and delivery continues.

## Dosage & Administration

Common research dosages for AOD-9604 range from 300–500 mcg per day, with some protocols using 500 mcg twice daily for enhanced effects. Due to its short half-life of approximately 20 minutes, frequent administration is required to maintain therapeutic peptide levels. Subcutaneous injection is the standard route of administration. AOD-9604 is often cycled in 8–12 week research protocols with breaks to assess metabolic response.

## Safety & Legal

AOD-9604 is intended for research purposes only. Its safety profile is generally favourable due to its selective action on lipolysis without the metabolic side effects of full-length hGH. However, comprehensive long-term human safety data are limited. The lyophilised powder should be stored at -20°C and reconstituted with bacteriostatic water immediately before use. Reconstituted solutions should be refrigerated and used within 7 days.


The identification of AOD-9604 as the minimal fragment of hGH retaining full lipolytic activity was the result of systematic structure-activity studies conducted in the 1990s. Researchers determined that the amino acid sequence 177–191 of hGH was both necessary and sufficient for its fat-burning effects, while the diabetogenic and growth-promoting activities resided elsewhere in the molecule. This discovery opened the door to developing a targeted metabolic agent that could selectively stimulate lipolysis without the side effects of systemic GH administration.

The clinical development of AOD-9604 included phase 2 trials that evaluated its safety and efficacy for weight loss. A randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled study involving 300 overweight subjects showed that AOD-9604 300 mcg daily produced a statistically significant reduction in total body fat and waist circumference compared to placebo after 12 weeks, with an excellent safety profile and no effects on blood glucose or IGF-1 levels. These results were encouraging but were not sufficient to support FDA approval, and the peptide remains in research use rather than clinical practice.

An important consideration for AOD-9604 research is its very short half-life (approximately 20 minutes). This rapid clearance necessitates frequent administration — typically twice daily for research protocols — to maintain therapeutic peptide levels. Some researchers have explored sustained-release formulations or extended infusion protocols to overcome this limitation, though subcutaneous administration remains the standard approach.

When sourcing AOD-9604 for research, purity and identity are critical. The peptide should be ≥98% pure as verified by HPLC analysis. UK researchers should ensure their supplier provides batch-specific analytical data. The lyophilised powder is stable for extended periods at -20°C but should be protected from moisture and light during storage.

name: Retatrutide
description: Retatrutide is a novel triple agonist targeting GIP, GLP-1, and glucagon receptors developed for obesity and type 2 diabetes. It has demonstrated unprecedented weight reduction in clinical trials, with patients achieving up to 24% body weight loss at higher doses.
mechanism: Triple receptor agonist at GIP, GLP-1, and glucagon receptors. GIP and GLP-1 agonism drive insulin secretion and satiety while glucagon agonism increases energy expenditure through hepatic fat oxidation and thermogenesis.
longDescription:
## Overview

Retatrutide (LY3437943) represents the next frontier in incretin-based therapeutics as a novel triple agonist simultaneously targeting the GIP, GLP-1, and glucagon receptors. Developed by Eli Lilly, this peptide has captured significant scientific and commercial attention following clinical trial results that demonstrated unprecedented weight reduction — with patients achieving up to 24.2% body weight loss at higher doses over 48 weeks, surpassing results seen with any previously studied pharmacological intervention for obesity. Retatrutide's triple mechanism adds glucagon receptor agonism to the established GIP/GLP-1 dual agonist framework, introducing increased energy expenditure through hepatic fat oxidation and thermogenesis. UK researchers and metabolic specialists are closely following retatrutide's development for its potential applications in severe obesity, type 2 diabetes, NAFLD/NASH, and broader metabolic syndrome. The peptide's novelty, potency, and unique mechanism make it one of the most exciting developments in metabolic research.

## Mechanism of Action

Retatrutide acts as a balanced triple receptor agonist at the glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) receptor, the glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor, and the glucagon receptor (GCGR). GIP and GLP-1 agonism work synergistically to promote glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, slow gastric emptying, and enhance satiety through central pathways — similar to the mechanism of tirzepatide. The critical addition of glucagon receptor agonism distinguishes retatrutide by directly stimulating hepatic fat oxidation, increasing energy expenditure, and promoting thermogenesis in adipose tissue. Glucagon also contributes to lipolysis and ketogenesis, creating a more comprehensive metabolic effect that addresses both energy intake (through satiety) and energy expenditure (through thermogenesis). The resulting metabolic profile produces weight loss that substantially exceeds what is achievable with dual agonism alone.

## Research & Clinical Data

The phase 2 clinical trial of retatrutide published in The Lancet in 2023 produced striking results: at the 12 mg once-weekly dose, participants achieved mean weight reductions of 24.2% after 48 weeks, with approximately 50% of patients achieving ≥25% weight loss — an effect size approaching that of bariatric surgery. Improvements in HbA1c, fasting glucose, lipid profiles, and blood pressure were also reported, with the highest dose group showing the most robust metabolic improvements. Retatrutide is currently in phase 3 clinical development (TRIUMPH programme) for obesity and type 2 diabetes, with ongoing studies in NAFLD/NASH and cardiovascular outcomes.

## Dosage & Administration

In clinical trials, retatrutide was administered as a once-weekly subcutaneous injection with dose escalation from 1 mg progressing through 2 mg, 4 mg, 6 mg, 8 mg, and up to 12 mg weekly. The half-life of approximately 6 days supports once-weekly administration. Lyophilised retatrutide requires reconstitution with bacteriostatic water prior to use and should be stored under refrigeration at 2–8°C after reconstitution.

## Safety & Legal

Retatrutide is strictly for research purposes. Gastrointestinal effects including nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, and constipation are the most commonly reported events, consistent with the GLP-1 agonist class. Increased heart rate has also been observed, consistent with glucagon receptor activation. The long-term safety profile continues to be evaluated in ongoing phase 3 trials. Researchers should source from verified peptide suppliers with ≥98% purity and clear labelling for investigational use only.


The development of retatrutide represents the culmination of decades of research into incretin biology and peptide engineering. Eli Lilly's scientists meticulously optimised the peptide sequence to achieve balanced agonism at all three receptors — GIP, GLP-1, and glucagon — in a single molecule. The engineering challenge was considerable: each receptor requires different interactions with the peptide, and maintaining potency at one receptor can compromise activity at another. The final molecule achieves near-equipotent activation of all three targets.

The addition of glucagon agonism is the key innovation distinguishing retatrutide from tirzepatide. Glucagon acts on the liver to stimulate glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis, and fatty acid oxidation — processes that increase energy expenditure. This thermogenic effect is believed to account for the additional weight loss observed with retatrutide compared to dual GIP/GLP-1 agonists. However, glucagon agonism also increases heart rate and can cause hyperglycaemia if not balanced by GLP-1 activity, making the precise ratio of activities critical for the safety profile.

The phase 2 results published in The Lancet in June 2023 were met with both excitement and caution in the medical community. While the 24.2% mean weight loss at 48 weeks is unprecedented for a pharmacological intervention — approaching the efficacy of bariatric surgery — it is important to note that these results were from a relatively small phase 2 study. The phase 3 TRIUMPH programme, expected to involve thousands of participants, will provide definitive evidence on efficacy and safety.

For researchers considering retatrutide studies, the peptide's 6-day half-life supports once-weekly dosing. Dose escalation from 1 mg to 12 mg weekly should follow established protocols to assess tolerability. Retatrutide is available from both UK and international suppliers, but researchers should verify that their source provides independent analytical testing given the peptide's novelty and complexity.

name: CJC-1295
description: CJC-1295 is a synthetic analogue of growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) with Drug Affinity Complex (DAC) technology that extends its half-life. It stimulates pulsatile GH release and raises IGF-1 levels for prolonged periods after a single injection.
mechanism: GHRH receptor agonist. Binds to pituitary GHRH receptors stimulating GH synthesis and pulsatile release. The DAC modification binds to albumin, extending plasma half-life to approximately 6-8 days.
longDescription:
## Overview

CJC-1295 is a synthetic analogue of growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) that incorporates proprietary Drug Affinity Complex (DAC) technology to dramatically extend its circulating half-life. This modified tetrasubstituted GHRH(1-29) peptide represents a significant advancement in growth hormone secretagogue research, as a single injection can stimulate pulsatile GH release and elevate IGF-1 levels for 6–8 days. By binding to GHRH receptors on pituitary somatotrophs, CJC-1295 enhances the body's natural GH production without bypassing the endogenous regulatory feedback mechanisms — a key distinction from synthetic GH administration. UK researchers in endocrinology, anti-aging, and sports science have utilised CJC-1295 extensively for research into muscle growth, bone density, recovery, and age-related GH decline. Its long-acting profile makes it particularly valuable for protocols requiring sustained GH pulse enhancement.

## Mechanism of Action

CJC-1295 acts as a potent agonist at the GHRH receptor, a G-protein-coupled receptor on pituitary somatotrophs. Binding activates the cAMP/PKA signalling cascade, which stimulates both GH gene transcription and the pulsatile release of pre-formed GH from secretory granules. The DAC modification involves coupling the peptide to maleimidopropionic acid, which binds covalently to cysteine-34 of circulating albumin. This albumin binding creates a circulating reservoir that slowly releases active peptide, extending the plasma half-life from minutes to approximately 6–8 days. Unlike GHRP compounds that act through the ghrelin receptor, CJC-1295 works through the physiological GHRH pathway, preserving the natural pulsatility of GH secretion.

## Research & Clinical Data

Clinical studies of CJC-1295 have demonstrated sustained elevations of GH and IGF-1 lasting up to 11 days after a single dose. A phase I/II trial showed that a single 60–120 mcg/kg dose increased GH levels for 6–9 days and IGF-1 levels remained elevated for up to 11 days. The peptide was generally well tolerated, with mild injection site reactions being the most common adverse event. Research continues into its potential applications in age-related GH decline, muscle wasting conditions, and bone density preservation. The pharmacokinetic profile of CJC-1295 makes it a compelling alternative to shorter-acting GHRH analogues like sermorelin.

## Dosage & Administration

The long half-life of CJC-1295 (6–8 days) supports once or twice weekly dosing in research protocols, typically 1–2 mg per week. For those accustomed to shorter-acting GHRH peptides, an equivalent protocol might involve 100–200 mcg twice daily of the non-DAC form (Mod GRF 1-29). CJC-1295 is administered via subcutaneous injection and should be reconstituted with bacteriostatic water. Reconstituted solutions should be refrigerated and used within 7–14 days.

## Safety & Legal

CJC-1295 is for research purposes only. Common reported effects include injection site reactions, transient flushing, and headache. Prolonged elevation of GH and IGF-1 may carry theoretical risks including acromegaly-like effects with chronic overuse. The peptide should be stored as lyophilised powder at -20°C.


CJC-1295 exemplifies the power of medicinal chemistry to transform peptide pharmacokinetics. The Drug Affinity Complex (DAC) technology involves conjugating a maleimide group to the peptide that forms a covalent bond with cysteine-34 on circulating albumin. Because albumin has a half-life of approximately 19 days, the peptide-albumin adduct creates a long-lasting circulating reservoir. This approach has been used successfully in other peptide therapeutics, including liraglutide (which uses a non-covalent albumin-binding fatty acid), but CJC-1295's covalent attachment provides an unusually sustained release profile.

The tetrasubstitution at key positions in CJC-1295 — specifically substitutions that enhance receptor binding and reduce DPP-IV degradation — further optimise the peptide for therapeutic use. The sequence YADAIFTNSYRKVLGQLSARKLLQDIMSR represents a significant modification of the native GHRH sequence that improves stability while maintaining full receptor agonist activity. These modifications collectively enable the once-weekly or twice-weekly dosing that distinguishes CJC-1295 from shorter-acting GHRH analogues.

Clinical studies demonstrated that CJC-1295 produces sustained GH and IGF-1 elevations. A single dose in healthy volunteers increased GH levels for 6–9 days and maintained elevated IGF-1 for up to 11 days. This gradual, sustained increase in GH/IGF-1 contrasts with the sharp pulses produced by acute GHRH administration and may have distinct biological effects that are still being characterised.

When using CJC-1295 in research, it is important to understand the difference between CJC-1295 with DAC (the long-acting form described here) and CJC-1295 without DAC (often called Mod GRF 1-29). The latter is essentially identical to sermorelin and requires twice-daily dosing. Researchers should ensure they are using the correct form for their experimental design. UK suppliers carry both forms, and clear labelling should confirm which version is provided.

name: IGF-1 LR3
description: IGF-1 LR3 is a synthetic 83-amino acid analogue of human IGF-1 with an arginine substitution at position three and an N-terminal extension. It has significantly reduced binding to IGF-binding proteins, resulting in a much longer half-life and greater bioavailability than native IGF-1.
mechanism: IGF-1 receptor agonist. Promotes protein synthesis, nitrogen retention, and cell proliferation. The LR3 modification reduces IGFBP binding affinity by 1000-fold, extending half-life to approximately 20-30 hours vs 10-12 minutes for native IGF-1.
longDescription:
## Overview

IGF-1 LR3 (Long Arginine 3 Insulin-like Growth Factor-1) is a synthetic 83-amino acid analogue of human IGF-1 engineered for dramatically enhanced bioavailability and half-life. The modification involves substituting the third amino acid (arginine for glutamic acid) and adding an N-terminal extension (MFPAMPLSSLFNGPRTLCGAELVDALQFVCGDRGFYFNKPTGYGSSSRRAPQTGIVDECCFRSCDLRRLEMYCAPLKPAKSA), creating a peptide with approximately 1000-fold reduced affinity for IGF-binding proteins (IGFBPs). Normally, native IGF-1 is rapidly sequestered by IGFBPs and cleared within 10–12 minutes. IGF-1 LR3 overcomes this limitation, achieving a half-life of 20–30 hours and significantly greater bioavailability at target tissues. This makes IGF-1 LR3 a powerful research tool for studying muscle growth, recovery, insulin sensitivity, nerve regeneration, and anti-aging pathways. UK researchers in sports science and regenerative medicine have utilised IGF-1 LR3 to investigate protein synthesis and tissue repair mechanisms.

## Mechanism of Action

IGF-1 LR3 exerts its effects by binding to the IGF-1 receptor (IGF-1R), a receptor tyrosine kinase that activates the PI3K/Akt and MAPK/ERK signalling cascades. This leads to increased protein synthesis through mTOR activation, enhanced nitrogen retention, inhibited protein breakdown (through FoxO phosphorylation), and promotion of cell proliferation and survival. The reduced binding to IGFBPs (particularly IGFBP-3, the most abundant circulating binding protein) means that a much larger proportion of administered peptide remains free and biologically active — a critical advantage over native IGF-1. IGF-1 LR3 also exhibits some cross-reactivity with the insulin receptor, though with lower affinity, potentially contributing to improved insulin sensitivity in certain research contexts.

## Research & Clinical Data

Research studies have demonstrated that IGF-1 LR3 significantly enhances muscle protein synthesis and promotes myoblast proliferation and differentiation in vitro. Animal studies report improved muscle regeneration following injury, enhanced nerve regeneration after peripheral nerve damage, and increased bone density in models of osteoporosis. The extended half-life of 20–30 hours allows for once or twice daily dosing schedules, compared to the impractical continuous infusion required for native IGF-1. Clinical applications for IGF-1 deficiency, muscle wasting diseases, and metabolic disorders continue to be explored.

## Dosage & Administration

Common research dosages for IGF-1 LR3 range from 20–60 mcg per day for general research purposes, with post-workout protocols of 40–80 mcg used in muscle growth studies. Due to its 20–30 hour half-life, once or twice daily administration is sufficient. Subcutaneous or intramuscular injection are both used in research protocols. The peptide should be reconstituted with bacteriostatic water (or dilute acetic acid for optimal stability) and stored at 2–8°C after reconstitution.

## Safety & Legal

IGF-1 LR3 is for research purposes only. Potential risks associated with IGF-1 pathway activation include hypoglycaemia (due to insulin receptor cross-reactivity), acromegaly-like effects with chronic use, and theoretical concerns regarding cell proliferation in susceptible tissues. The lyophilised powder should be stored at -20°C and protected from light and moisture.


The engineering of IGF-1 LR3 addressed a fundamental challenge in growth factor therapeutics: the rapid clearance of IGF-1 due to its high-affinity binding to a family of six binding proteins (IGFBPs) present in plasma at molar excess. Native IGF-1 has a half-life of only 10–12 minutes because it is rapidly sequestered by IGFBPs, primarily IGFBP-3. The R3 substitution (arginine for glutamic acid at position 3) reduces affinity for all IGFBPs by approximately 1000-fold, while the N-terminal extension further enhances this property.

The implications of this reduced IGFBP binding extend beyond pharmacokinetics. IGFBPs not only transport IGF-1 in the circulation but also modulate its interaction with the IGF-1 receptor. Some IGFBPs can potentiate or inhibit IGF-1 signalling in a tissue-specific manner. By circumventing these regulatory proteins, IGF-1 LR3 provides a more direct and uniform activation of the IGF-1 receptor across different target tissues — which may be advantageous for some research applications but could also bypass important physiological regulatory mechanisms.

Research has demonstrated that IGF-1 LR3 is particularly effective in promoting muscle protein synthesis. Studies in animal models show increased muscle mass and fibre cross-sectional area with IGF-1 LR3 treatment, even in the absence of resistance exercise. The peptide also promotes satellite cell activation and myonuclear accretion, suggesting it may enhance the regenerative capacity of skeletal muscle — a finding with implications for sarcopenia and muscle-wasting conditions.

For research use, IGF-1 LR3 requires careful handling due to its susceptibility to oxidation and aggregation. Reconstitution with dilute acetic acid (rather than bacteriostatic water) can improve stability. The peptide should be stored at -20°C as lyophilised powder and used promptly after reconstitution. UK researchers should source from vendors who provide purity analysis and storage recommendations specific to this peptide.

name: IGF-1 DES
description: IGF-1 DES (Des(1-3) IGF-1) is a truncated 67-amino acid analogue of human IGF-1 lacking the first three N-terminal amino acids. This deletion dramatically reduces binding to all six IGF-binding proteins, producing a peptide with greater potency and bioavailability at target tissues compared to full-length IGF-1 despite its significantly shorter half-life.
mechanism: IGF-1 receptor agonist with minimal IGFBP binding. Acts via PI3K/Akt and MAPK/ERK pathways to promote protein synthesis, cell proliferation, and tissue repair. The N-terminal truncation eliminates virtually all IGFBP sequestration, making a higher fraction of the peptide available to activate IGF-1R.
longDescription:
## Overview

IGF-1 DES (Des(1-3) Insulin-like Growth Factor-1) is a 67-amino acid truncated analogue of human IGF-1, formed by removing the N-terminal tripeptide Gly-Pro-Glu. This seemingly minor modification has profound consequences for IGF-binding protein (IGFBP) affinity — the deletion reduces binding to all six known IGFBPs by several orders of magnitude, producing a peptide with substantially greater free fraction and tissue bioavailability than either native IGF-1 or the extended-analogue IGF-1 LR3. Unlike IGF-1 LR3, which achieves its extended half-life through retained IGFBP binding and slow dissociation, IGF-1 DES bypasses the binding protein system almost entirely. This makes IGF-1 DES a uniquely potent research tool for studying IGF-1 receptor signalling without the buffering effects of binding proteins. Researchers in the UK and internationally use IGF-1 DES for investigations into muscle hypertrophy, tissue repair, metabolic regulation, and cell proliferation pathways.

## Mechanism of Action

IGF-1 DES activates the IGF-1 receptor (IGF-1R), a transmembrane receptor tyrosine kinase, triggering downstream PI3K/Akt and MAPK/ERK signalling cascades. Akt activation leads to mTOR-mediated protein synthesis and inhibition of protein breakdown via FoxO phosphorylation. The key distinction from native IGF-1 and IGF-1 LR3 is the almost complete absence of IGFBP binding. Native IGF-1 is rapidly sequestered by IGFBPs, particularly IGFBP-3, which act as carrier proteins that control IGF-1 distribution and half-life. IGF-1 LR3 reduces this binding by ~1000-fold. IGF-1 DES eliminates it almost entirely. The consequence is extraordinarily rapid receptor activation at the expense of a very short circulating half-life (10–15 minutes). This pharmacokinetic profile makes IGF-1 DES particularly suitable for acute, high-peak signalling research rather than sustained tonic stimulation.

faq.question: What is IGF-1 DES used for in research?
faq.answer: IGF-1 DES is researched for muscle hypertrophy, tissue repair, metabolic studies, and cell proliferation pathways. Its minimal IGFBP binding makes it a uniquely potent tool for studying IGF-1 receptor signalling without binding protein interference.

faq.question: How does IGF-1 DES differ from IGF-1 LR3?
faq.answer: IGF-1 DES has the first three N-terminal amino acids removed, virtually eliminating all IGFBP binding. IGF-1 LR3 uses an arginine substitution and N-terminal extension to reduce binding ~1000-fold. DES has greater potency per dose but a much shorter half-life (10-15 min vs 20-30 hours for LR3).

faq.answer: Common research dosages range from 30-60 mcg daily for general protocols, with 40-80 mcg post-workout used in muscle growth studies. The very short half-life (10-15 minutes) supports per-workout or twice-daily administration.

name: Tesamorelin
description: Tesamorelin is a synthetic 44-amino acid analogue of human growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) approved for HIV-associated lipodystrophy. It restores pulsatile GH secretion and raises IGF-1 levels with a favourable safety profile.
mechanism: GHRH receptor agonist. Stimulates endogenous GH synthesis and pulsatile release from pituitary somatotrophs. Unlike CJC-1295, it does not have DAC modification and has a shorter duration of action.
longDescription:
## Overview

Tesamorelin is a synthetic 44-amino acid analogue of human growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) that is approved by the FDA and EMA for the treatment of HIV-associated lipodystrophy under the brand name Egrifta. As a full-length GHRH analogue, tesamorelin restores the natural pulsatile pattern of GH secretion, raising IGF-1 levels while maintaining the body's endogenous feedback mechanisms — a fundamentally different approach from exogenous GH administration. Unlike CJC-1295, tesamorelin does not incorporate DAC modification and therefore has a shorter duration of action requiring daily administration. However, it offers the advantage of being the only GHRH analogue with completed phase 3 clinical programmes and regulatory approval, providing a well-characterised safety and efficacy profile. UK researchers have investigated tesamorelin for its ability to reduce visceral adipose tissue, improve metabolic parameters, and its potential applications in anti-aging, cognitive function, and general GH deficiency.

## Mechanism of Action

Tesamorelin acts as a full agonist at the GHRH receptor on pituitary somatotrophs. Binding to this G-protein-coupled receptor activates the cAMP/PKA signalling cascade, stimulating both GH gene transcription and the pulsatile release of stored GH from secretory granules. Importantly, tesamorelin preserves the endogenous feedback loop involving IGF-1 and somatostatin, meaning GH release remains under physiological regulation. This is a key safety advantage over exogenous GH administration, which suppresses the HPA axis and can lead to iatrogenic acromegaly. The full 44-amino acid sequence provides complete biological activity equivalent to endogenous GHRH, with a plasma half-life of approximately 30–60 minutes requiring daily administration.

## Research & Clinical Data

FDA and EMA approval for tesamorelin was based on two pivotal phase 3 trials in HIV-associated lipodystrophy involving over 800 patients. Treatment with 2 mg daily for 26 weeks resulted in a statistically significant reduction in visceral adipose tissue (approximately 15–20% reduction from baseline) as measured by CT scan, along with improvements in triglyceride levels and cardiovascular risk markers. Open-label extension studies demonstrated sustained effects over 12 months with no loss of efficacy. Research into tesamorelin for cognitive function in mild cognitive impairment, age-related visceral fat accumulation, and growth hormone deficiency continues in ongoing studies.

## Dosage & Administration

The standard research dosage for tesamorelin is 2 mg administered once daily via subcutaneous injection. The relatively short half-life of 30–60 minutes necessitates daily administration. Tesamorelin is reconstituted with sterile water for injection and should be used immediately or within 24 hours when refrigerated.

## Safety & Legal

Tesamorelin is approved for clinical use in HIV-associated lipodystrophy but is for research purposes in other contexts. Common side effects include injection site reactions, joint pain, flushing, and peripheral oedema. Long-term safety data are available from clinical trial extension studies up to 12 months. The lyophilised powder should be stored at 2–8°C and protected from light.


Tesamorelin's regulatory approval by the FDA in 2010 for HIV-associated lipodystrophy marked a significant milestone in GHRH-based therapeutics. HIV lipodystrophy is a condition characterised by visceral fat accumulation, insulin resistance, and dyslipidaemia — metabolic abnormalities that increase cardiovascular risk in the HIV population. Tesamorelin's ability to reduce visceral adipose tissue by 15–20% while improving lipid profiles addressed an important unmet medical need.

The development of tesamorelin involved extensive preclinical and clinical characterisation. Unlike the shorter GHRH fragments (sermorelin), tesamorelin is a full-length 44-amino acid analogue that more closely recapitulates the biological activity of endogenous GHRH. The peptide was designed to resist DPP-IV degradation through strategic amino acid substitutions while maintaining full receptor binding and activation.

Beyond HIV lipodystrophy, tesamorelin has been investigated for age-related visceral fat accumulation. A study in healthy older adults with abdominal obesity showed that tesamorelin reduced visceral adipose tissue and improved the ratio of visceral to subcutaneous fat. Research into its effects on cognition in mild cognitive impairment (MCI) has shown preliminary evidence of improved cognitive function, possibly mediated through IGF-1's neurotrophic effects on the hippocampus.

The practical aspects of tesamorelin research are straightforward: the standard dose is 2 mg once daily by subcutaneous injection. The peptide should be reconstituted with sterile water for injection and used immediately or stored at 2–8°C for up to 24 hours. Given its approval status in HIV lipodystrophy, tesamorelin has a well-characterised safety profile that includes injection site reactions, joint pain, and peripheral oedema as the most common adverse events.

faq.question: What is Tesamorelin used for?
faq.answer: Tesamorelin is approved for HIV-associated lipodystrophy (visceral fat accumulation) and researched for anti-aging, GH stimulation, cognition, and visceral fat reduction in non-HIV populations. It is a full-length GHRH analogue that restores natural GH pulsatility.

name: Sermorelin
description: Sermorelin is a synthetic 29-amino acid fragment of human growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH). It acts as a GHRH agonist to stimulate endogenous GH release and is used in diagnostics and anti-aging therapy as a GH secretagogue.
mechanism: GHRH receptor agonist. Binds specifically to GHRH receptors on pituitary somatotrophs, stimulating GH synthesis and release. The truncated 1-29 sequence retains full biological activity of the full 44-amino acid GHRH.
longDescription:
## Overview

Sermorelin is a synthetic 29-amino acid fragment of human growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH), corresponding to the bioactive N-terminal 1–29 sequence that retains full biological activity of the native 44-amino acid hormone. Marketed under the brand name Geref and also known as GRF 1-29, sermorelin has been used clinically in diagnostic testing for growth hormone deficiency in children and as a GH secretagogue in anti-aging research. Its shorter peptide chain compared to full-length GHRH (tesamorelin) makes it simpler to synthesise and more cost-effective, while maintaining the ability to stimulate endogenous GH release through the physiological GHRH pathway. Unlike GHRP compounds that act via the ghrelin receptor, sermorelin works through the natural GHRH receptor, preserving the body's own regulatory mechanisms. UK researchers have utilised sermorelin in endocrine research, anti-aging protocols, and combinations with GHRP compounds for synergistic GH pulse generation.

## Mechanism of Action

Sermorelin binds specifically to GHRH receptors on anterior pituitary somatotrophs, G-protein-coupled receptors that activate adenylate cyclase and the cAMP/PKA signalling cascade. This stimulates GH gene transcription and promotes the pulsatile release of pre-synthesised GH from secretory granules. The 1-29 sequence encompasses the receptor-binding and activating domains of full-length GHRH, while lacking the C-terminal region that contributes to plasma stability. As a result, sermorelin has a very short plasma half-life of approximately 12–20 minutes and is rapidly degraded by dipeptidyl peptidase IV (DPP-IV). The preservation of pulsatile GH release and intact IGF-1 feedback distinguishes it from exogenous GH administration.

## Research & Clinical Data

Sermorelin is FDA-approved as a diagnostic agent for evaluating GH secretory capacity in children with short stature. Clinical research has demonstrated that twice-daily sermorelin administration can increase GH and IGF-1 levels in adults with relative GH deficiency. Studies combining sermorelin with GHRP-2 or GHRP-6 show synergistic GH release comparable to that seen with insulin tolerance testing. Research into sermorelin for age-related GH decline, muscle preservation, and metabolic health continues, with some studies suggesting improvements in body composition, bone density, and quality of life.

## Dosage & Administration

Due to its short half-life of 12–20 minutes, sermorelin is typically administered twice daily at 100–200 mcg per dose, or 200 mcg twice daily in more intensive protocols. Subcutaneous injection is standard. Sermorelin is often combined with a GHRP (e.g., GHRP-2 or GHRP-6) to produce a synergistic GH pulse that resembles the body's natural secretory pattern. Reconstitution with bacteriostatic water is standard, and solutions should be refrigerated at 2–8°C.

## Safety & Legal

Sermorelin is for research purposes. As a GHRH analogue, it has a favourable safety profile with common effects including injection site reactions, transient flushing, and headache. Because it works through the endogenous GH axis, the risk of acromegaly-like effects is theoretically lower than with exogenous GH. The lyophilised powder should be stored at -20°C for optimal stability.


Sermorelin's history as an FDA-approved diagnostic agent for GH deficiency provides a strong foundation for its use in research. Approved as Geref in the 1990s, sermorelin was used to assess pituitary GH secretory capacity in children with growth disorders. The diagnostic test involves measuring GH levels at intervals after a sermorelin bolus, with peak GH levels below a threshold indicating GH deficiency. This clinical validation established the safety and reliability of sermorelin as a GH secretagogue.

The combination of sermorelin with a GHRP (most commonly GHRP-2 or ipamorelin) has become a standard protocol in GH research. The synergistic effect — sermorelin stimulates GH synthesis while GHRP enhances GH release — produces a GH pulse that exceeds what either compound can achieve alone. This combination is often described as a "GHRP punch" followed by a GHRH-mediated sustained release, mimicking the body's natural GH secretory pattern more closely than either agent alone.

For UK researchers interested in GH physiology, sermorelin offers several practical advantages. Its short half-life (12–20 minutes) means that frequent administration provides a research model of pulsatile GH secretion. The twice-daily dosing schedule (100–200 mcg per injection) is manageable and allows for studies of GH-dependent metabolic processes under controlled conditions. The peptide is well tolerated with minimal side effects.

When sourcing sermorelin in the UK, researchers should distinguish between the standard peptide and the modified forms. The non-DAC version (often called Mod GRF 1-29) is functionally equivalent to sermorelin, while CJC-1295 with DAC has a much longer half-life. UK suppliers including United Peptides and UK Peptides offer both forms, and careful labelling ensures researchers receive the correct product for their experimental design.

faq.answer: Sermorelin is researched for growth hormone stimulation, anti-aging, muscle growth, sleep quality improvement, and GH deficiency diagnostics. It is a 29-amino acid GHRH analogue that stimulates endogenous GH release through the natural GHRH pathway.

faq.answer: Sermorelin is a 29-amino acid fragment of GHRH, while tesamorelin is the full 44-amino acid sequence. Both work through the same GHRH receptor, but the shorter sermorelin sequence results in a shorter half-life (12–20 minutes vs 30–60 minutes for tesamorelin).
